Long before the mountains had names, there lived a lonely spirit named Aethel. Aethel was woven from the mist of the high peaks and the chill of the morning air. She had no voice of her own, only the rustle of pine needles and the shifting of loose scree.

One evening, a shepherd climbed higher than usual, seeking a lost lamb. To soothe his weary heart, he began to sing. His voice was rich and warm, a golden thread winding through the desolate crags. Aethel listened, mesmerized. She had never known such beauty.

Desperate to hold onto the sound, she reached out and caught the final notes of his song in her misty hands. But a spirit cannot hold a mortal's gift forever without giving something back. As the shepherd turned to leave, Aethel tossed the notes back to him, mimicking his melody perfectly.

Startled, the shepherd called out, "Who is there?"

Aethel, longing to communicate, could only throw his words back: "Who is there?"

To this day, Aethel wanders the canyons and valleys. She still has no voice of her own, but she eagerly waits for travelers, gratefully returning their words in the phenomenon we now call the echo.

Her constant companion, the great obsidian hound Porthos, slept at her feet, his breathing the gentle night wind.

One evening, a dark comet hurtled through the silent void, threatening to shatter the tranquility of the mortal realm below. Awakened by the disturbance, Porthos sprang into action. He grabbed a bundle of Elara’s brightest starlight threads in his jaws and gave chase.

Porthos successfully chased the comet away into the deep unknown, but the sky was forever changed. Now, whenever the great hound patrols the borders of the night, his paws kick up the remnants of those frayed silver threads. Mortals look up and make wishes upon these fleeting streaks of light, marveling at the cosmic dust we now call shooting stars.
